mnemonics: Imagine the Byzantine Generals as a group of unreliable texters trying to coordinate a night out. They need a system (like a group chat with read receipts) where they can all agree on a single plan, even if some are lying about their availability or trying to sabotage the plans.
keyPoints: The Byzantine Generals Problem is a logical dilemma that illustrates the difficulty of achieving consensus in a distributed system when there are unreliable or malicious actors.
It proves that for a system to be fault-tolerant, a consensus must be reachable even if up to one-third of the participants are malicious or faulty.

Blockchain solves this problem through its consensus mechanisms (like Proof-of-Work or Proof-of-Stake), which allow a decentralized network to agree on the state of a shared ledger without a trusted central authority.
commonMistakes: Thinking the problem is only about stopping malicious actors; it is equally about handling non-responsive or faulty components that fail to communicate.
Believing a simple majority (51%) is always sufficient to achieve consensus; the problem shows specific conditions and thresholds (like the 1/3 rule) are required for a robust solution.
